statement: Croatia became the 28th EU member state on 1 July 2013, following a decade-long accession process. The accession referendum (22 January 2012) produced 66.3% in favour on a turnout of only 43.5%. Croatia's GDP per capita at accession was approximately 60% of the EU average. Since joining, Croatia has experienced mixed economic outcomes: GDP growth averaged 2.5% per year (2014–2024), FDI increased modestly, and emigration to wealthier EU states accelerated (approximately 300,000 Croats left, ~7% of the population). Croatia adopted the euro and joined Schengen in January 2023. The Croatian experience illustrates both the opportunities (market access, structural funds, euro stability) and risks (brain drain, delayed convergence) of accession for a small, peripheral economy.
source_name: European Commission — Croatia post-accession monitoring reports; Croatian Bureau of Statistics
caveats: Croatia's pre-accession economic structure (war-damaged, post-socialist transition) differs fundamentally from Iceland's. The emigration problem partly reflects Croatia's lower wage levels relative to Western Europe — Iceland's wages are already among Europe's highest, reducing emigration risk. The low referendum turnout raises questions about democratic legitimacy.

statement: UK goods trade with the EU declined significantly following Brexit. UK exports to the EU fell by approximately 15% in real terms between 2019 and 2024, while imports from the EU fell by approximately 10%. The UK's goods trade deficit with the EU widened from £94 billion (2019) to approximately £120 billion (2024). Non-tariff barriers — customs declarations, rules of origin documentation, sanitary and phytosanitary checks — proved more costly than tariffs for many businesses. The UK government's own analysis estimated that Brexit reduced long-run UK GDP by approximately 4% compared to continued EU membership. Services trade was less affected, as the UK maintained some market access through the Trade and Cooperation Agreement (TCA).
source_name: UK Office for National Statistics — Trade statistics; Office for Budget Responsibility — Brexit impact assessment
caveats: Brexit trade impacts are difficult to isolate from COVID-19 effects (both occurred in 2020–2021). The 4% GDP reduction estimate is contested by some economists. The UK-EU TCA provides a framework not available to countries without any EU relationship, so the comparison with a hypothetical Iceland-without-EEA scenario is imperfect.

statement: Foreign direct investment into the UK declined markedly following the 2016 Brexit vote. FDI inflows fell from an average of £50 billion per year (2013–2016) to approximately £30 billion per year (2017–2024). The UK's share of total European FDI fell from approximately 25% to 15% over the same period, with Ireland, the Netherlands, and Germany absorbing much of the redirected investment. Financial services were particularly affected, with an estimated 7,000 jobs relocating from London to EU financial centres (Dublin, Frankfurt, Paris, Amsterdam) by 2024. However, the UK attracted significant investment in technology, life sciences, and renewable energy, suggesting that FDI effects were sector-specific rather than universal.
source_name: OECD — FDI statistics; EY European Attractiveness Survey; City of London Corporation reports
caveats: FDI figures are volatile and affected by large individual transactions. Global FDI trends (declining since 2016) affect the comparison. The UK's FDI decline may partly reflect maturation of the economy rather than Brexit alone. Iceland's FDI profile (dominated by energy-intensive industry) is very different from the UK's.

statement: The UK fishing industry, which was promised a post-Brexit 'sea of opportunity', has experienced mixed outcomes. Under the TCA, the UK regained approximately 25% of the quota previously fished by EU vessels in UK waters, phased in over 5.5 years (2021–2026). However, UK fish exports to the EU — which accounted for ~75% of UK fish exports — faced new customs checks, health certificates, and border delays, increasing costs by an estimated 10–15% and causing significant disruption to perishable exports. The UK fishing fleet's total catch value increased by approximately 5% between 2019 and 2024 (largely reflecting higher fish prices), but employment in the sector continued to decline. The Scottish fishing industry was particularly critical of the TCA's terms, calling them a 'betrayal'.
source_name: UK Marine Management Organisation — UK Sea Fisheries Statistics; House of Lords European Affairs Committee — Brexit and fisheries (2023)
caveats: The UK's fisheries represent approximately 0.1% of GDP, compared to Iceland's ~8% (direct + indirect). Iceland's fishing industry is far more efficient and internationally competitive. The UK's disappointing fisheries outcome reflects the political dynamics of the TCA negotiation (fisheries were traded against other priorities), not an inherent impossibility of managing fisheries outside the EU.

statement: Switzerland's relationship with the EU is governed by over 120 bilateral agreements negotiated since the Swiss rejected EEA membership in a 1992 referendum (50.3% No). This patchwork grants Switzerland access to parts of the single market (free movement of persons, certain services, mutual recognition) but not others (financial services, agricultural products). The system requires constant renegotiation and updating — the EU has grown frustrated with the complexity and in 2021 Switzerland unilaterally terminated negotiations for an Institutional Framework Agreement that would have streamlined the relationship. As of 2025, new negotiations are underway. The Swiss model is more cumbersome than the EEA but provides greater selectivity. It is generally considered unsuitable as a model for Iceland, which already has more comprehensive EU market access through the EEA.
source_name: Swiss Federal Department of Foreign Affairs — Bilateral agreements overview; European Commission — EU-Switzerland relations factsheet
caveats: Switzerland's economic weight and geographic centrality give it leverage that Iceland lacks. The Swiss financial sector's importance to the EU creates mutual interdependence. Iceland's EEA membership already provides more comprehensive single market access than Switzerland's bilateral agreements.

statement: Montenegro, with a population of approximately 620,000 (comparable to Iceland), opened EU accession negotiations in June 2012 — making it the most advanced Western Balkans candidate. As of 2025, all 33 negotiation chapters have been opened but only 3 provisionally closed, illustrating the difficulty of the accession process even for willing candidates. Montenegro's main challenges are rule of law, judicial reform, and organised crime — issues largely irrelevant to Iceland. However, Montenegro's experience shows that small countries face capacity constraints in managing 33 parallel negotiation chapters simultaneously. Montenegro's small civil service struggles with the administrative burden, a challenge Iceland (with an even smaller bureaucracy) would share.
caveats: Montenegro's slow progress reflects specific political and governance challenges unrelated to Iceland's situation. Iceland's 2010–2013 negotiation experience showed faster progress: 27 chapters opened and 11 provisionally closed in 3 years, reflecting Iceland's high alignment with EU standards through the EEA. Resuming negotiations from the 2013 position could shorten the timeline significantly.

statement: Greenland is the only territory to have left the European Community (now EU), withdrawing on 1 February 1985 after a 1982 referendum in which 53% voted to leave. Greenland had joined the EEC in 1973 as part of Denmark, despite a local majority voting against. The primary motivation for withdrawal was fisheries — Greenlanders objected to European vessels fishing in their waters under the Common Fisheries Policy. After withdrawal, Greenland negotiated an Overseas Countries and Territories (OCT) association with the EU, including a fisheries partnership agreement that provides EU access to Greenlandic waters in exchange for annual payments (~€18 million per year as of 2024). Greenland's experience is directly relevant to Iceland as both are Arctic fishing nations, though Greenland's withdrawal occurred from within the EC rather than as a standalone accession decision.
source_name: Treaty amending the Treaties establishing the European Communities with regard to Greenland (1984); EU-Greenland fisheries partnership agreement
caveats: Greenland's situation differs from Iceland's in several ways: it is an autonomous territory of Denmark (not a sovereign state), has a much smaller population (~57,000), and its economy is almost entirely dependent on fisheries and Danish subsidies. The political and legal framework for a territory leaving vs a state joining are fundamentally different.

statement: Finland joined the EU on 1 January 1995 following a referendum in which 56.9% voted in favour (turnout 70.4%). Finland's accession is the most relevant Nordic precedent for Iceland. Pre-accession concerns included agricultural adjustment, loss of sovereignty, and neutrality. In the 30 years since joining, Finland's experience has been broadly positive: GDP per capita converged with the EU average, FDI inflows increased significantly, and the agricultural sector survived (though with significant restructuring and CAP support). Finland adopted the euro in 1999 and has been a committed eurozone member. However, Finland has also experienced specific downsides: the loss of independent monetary policy during the 2008 crisis limited adjustment options, and the Nokia decline (unrelated to EU membership but occurring within the eurozone constraint) highlighted the risks of currency union for small economies reliant on few sectors.
source_name: Statistics Finland — EU membership impact assessments; Eurostat — convergence indicators
caveats: Finland's pre-accession economy was more diversified than Iceland's (forestry, manufacturing, technology alongside natural resources). Finland's land border with Russia adds a security dimension absent from Iceland's calculus. The Nordic EU members (Finland, Sweden, Denmark) have had quite different experiences, complicating the use of any single country as a model.
